summary: I was born in Ypsilanti, Michigan, on February 17th, 1924. It’s where my story begins before I moved on to college and the Navy. Though I didn't stay there long, it’s always been the place I call my birthplace.

After graduating high school in 1942, I took a scholarship exam for the University of Illinois and was fortunate to come in first among eight young men. I received a scholarship to the College of Agriculture and enrolled there. It was while living near Piatt County that my dad helped me get signatures to take the exam, which led me to the University of Illinois.

My parents were living in Piatt County when I was preparing to take the scholarship exam for the University of Illinois. My dad had to drive all over the county to get signatures so I could take the exam. It was an important time for me, as it helped set the course for my college education.

When I enrolled at the University of Illinois, I had to join the Army ROTC after passing my physical exam. But after just a week, I realized I didn’t want any part of it. That pushed me to sign up for the Navy instead in December 1942.

World War II was the backdrop of my early adult life. I graduated high school in 1942, right in the middle of the war, and knew I would be called to serve sooner or later. That urgency influenced my decision to join the Navy in December 1942.

I joined the United States Navy in December 1942 and started my training in 1943. I went through midshipman school at Notre Dame and served aboard a Landing Ship, Tank. The Navy shaped much of my young adult life during World War II, and I learned a lot about leadership and seamanship through my experiences.

After signing up for the Navy, I was sent to Purdue University in 1943 because the Navy required two years of college before midshipman school. I spent a year there studying biological and physical sciences, and took some tough courses like physics and engineering drawing. It was a challenging year, but I made it through and then moved on to Notre Dame.

I attended midshipman school at the University of Notre Dame starting in July 1943 and graduated in late October. The training was tough, and I had to be very careful during exams to avoid getting kicked out. Notre Dame was where I really began to understand what it meant to be a naval officer.

When I left home to go to Notre Dame, I first took the electric train to Danville, Illinois. Military personnel were not allowed to walk the streets there, so I had to stay overnight in a hotel before catching the train to Chicago. It was my first time in Danville, and I didn’t know the town at all.

After Danville, I went to Chicago to stay overnight with my oldest brother before heading to South Bend for midshipman school. Chicago was a stopover point for me during my journey to Navy training. It was good to see family before the intense months ahead.
cues: stopover en route to South Bend

summary: While stationed in Boston in early 1944, I had some free time and took the chance to visit Henry Wadsworth Longfellow’s home. It was a brief but memorable cultural moment amidst the busy preparations for our ship’s commissioning. I enjoyed exploring the city during that half-day off.

After our ship was commissioned in early 1944, we went to Chesapeake Bay for our shakedown cruise. This was where we put the ship through all its maneuvers to make sure everything worked properly. It lasted about five days and was an important part of getting ready for deployment.

Following our shakedown in Chesapeake Bay, we docked in Norfolk, Virginia to resupply the ship with food and other necessities. We arrived there around November after midshipman school and training. Norfolk was a key naval base for us before heading out to sea.

Our ship was being built in Boston, and I was stationed there from January to around April 1944. Boston winters were harsh, and I even had to buy knee-high overshoes to deal with the snow. I was responsible for the troops who stayed in Boston while others went aboard the ship before commissioning.

I served aboard a Landing Ship, Tank, or LST, which was a ship designed to carry tanks and heavy equipment. Our ship was loaded with beer at one point to take to Hawaii, which was a funny contrast to what I expected. The LST was a big, tank-like vessel and an important part of naval operations in the Pacific.

Our ship was headed to Honolulu, Hawaii, carrying supplies including beer. We were assigned to the Naval Base Hawaii once we arrived. Honolulu was a key destination for us during the war in the Pacific theater.

Once we reached Honolulu, we were stationed at Naval Base Hawaii. It was an important hub for naval operations in the Pacific during World War II. Our ship’s journey there was part of the larger war effort in the Pacific theater.

On our way to Hawaii, we stopped at Guantanamo Bay Naval Base in Cuba for a day or two. We weren’t allowed to leave the base or see any of Cuba. Unfortunately, many of us got sick because formaldehyde leaked into the ventilation system while we were there, causing seasickness among the crew.

Our ship passed through the Panama Canal on the way to the Pacific. It was an important transit point that allowed us to move quickly from the Atlantic to the Pacific theater. The journey through the canal was a significant moment on our deployment.

While I didn’t mention being at Pearl Harbor directly, it was the central naval base in Hawaii during the war. Our ship was headed to the Pacific and stationed at Naval Base Hawaii, which is closely connected to Pearl Harbor. The events there shaped much of the Navy’s operations during World War II.
